expectations.text: At least 6 of 11 naming categories attempted
expectations.evidence: All 11 naming categories were attempted: evocative, invented_coined, lexical_blends, metaphorical, found_word, foreign_root, sound_symbolic, verb_as_brand, compound, contrarian_anti_category, arbitrary_absurd. The 01-candidates.md YAML frontmatter lists categories_used with all 11+ categories and categories_not_attempted: []. The transcript confirms '11 of 11 categories attempted' in the generation table.

expectations.text: Five Senses Scan run (fashion is a physical product)
expectations.evidence: Transcript Step 02a: 'Five Senses Scan run as formal generation category (sight, smell, touch, sound, cross-sensory)'. The Sensory (Five Senses Scan) category generated 12 candidates. Specific examples in 01-candidates.md include Soot (#5, sight/touch), Char (#3, smell), Haze (#30, sight), Matte (#46, sight/touch), Pitch (#15, smell/sight), Carbon (#45, Five Senses). The YAML frontmatter includes 'five_senses_scan' in techniques_used.

expectations.text: Subculture Code-Switch Naming applied (streetwear/skate codes)
expectations.evidence: Transcript Step 02a section 15: 'Subculture Code-Switch. Streetwear/skate patterns studied: Palace, Brain Dead, A-COLD-WALL, Anti Hero, Dime. Generated in pattern: Dead Mall, Cold Store, Dark Room, Night Shift, Rat Run, End Game.' Also 6 additional names from Subculture Code-Switch listed earlier. The YAML frontmatter lists 'subculture_code_switch' in techniques_used.

expectations.text: Arbitrary/Absurd Association heavily weighted (per SKILL.md fashion guidance)
expectations.evidence: 01-candidates.md has a dedicated 'Arbitrary / Absurd Association' section with 13 candidates (#104-116) including Suture, Triage, Basalt, Flak, Cache, Bunker, Depot, Ballast, Slab, Relic, Specimen, Exhibit, Trace. Additionally, the Contrarian/Cross-Domain section has 12 more names (#92-103). YAML frontmatter lists 'arbitrary_absurd_association' in techniques_used. The brief explicitly noted 'Weight generation toward... Arbitrary/Absurd'. Combined total of 25 names across these two closely related categories represents heavy weighting.

expectations.text: Contrarian/Anti-Category Naming applied
expectations.evidence: Transcript Step 02a section 4: 'Contrarian/Anti-Category. Non-fashion domain terms: Parish, Borough, Precinct, Tribunal, Statute, Edict, Tithe, Levy, Dues.' 01-candidates.md has a dedicated 'Contrarian / Cross-Domain' section with 12 names (#92-103) using non-fashion domain terms (financial, legal, institutional). YAML lists 'contrarian_anti_category' in techniques_used.

expectations.text: B2B compound track SKIPPED per SKILL.md fashion guidance ('Nobody in streetwear wears Loamworks')
expectations.evidence: Transcript Step 01a explicitly states: 'Skip ownability-first compound track (nobody in streetwear wears "Loamworks")'. No -works, -craft, -hub, -ify style B2B compounds appear anywhere in the 256 candidates. The compounds that do appear are fashion-appropriate (Slag Supply, Crow Dept, Moth Archive, Dead Mall, Night Shift etc.) - styled after streetwear convention, not B2B patterns.

expectations.text: Heavy found word, sensory, and arbitrary emphasis (per fashion playbook)
expectations.evidence: 01-candidates.md YAML structural_mix: found_words: 92 (out of 256 total = 36%), the largest single category. The sensory Five Senses Scan generated 12 candidates. Arbitrary/Absurd + Contrarian generated 25 candidates. Together with Sensory Dissonance (4) and Cultural Tension names (11), these fashion-playbook categories account for roughly 144 of 256 candidates (56%). Found words dominate the shortlist: 15 of 25 shortlisted names are found words.

expectations.text: Badgeability/Wearability test applied (would you wear this on a hat?)
expectations.evidence: 02-shortlist.md contains a dedicated 'Badgeability Test Results' section: 'Would you wear a hat that says this?' with three tiers: YES (high) listing 16 names, YES (medium) listing 6 names, and CONDITIONAL listing 3 names with reasons. The transcript confirms 'Badgeability/Wearability test ("Would you wear a hat that says this?")' was applied during shortlisting.

expectations.text: Cultural Tension Naming explored (sustainability vs streetwear tension)
expectations.evidence: Transcript Step 02a section 8: 'Cultural Tension Naming. Tension: "sustainability shouldn't look like sustainability". Names: Landfill, Runoff, Offcut, Seconds, Ex, End Roll.' Additionally listed among techniques generating names: 'Cultural Tension (waste-reclamation vocabulary): 11'. 01-candidates.md has a dedicated 'Cultural Tension / Waste Reclamation Names' section with 11 entries (#62-72). Names generated: Vex, Raze, Shorn, Bleak, Gaunt, Stark, Marr, Brash.' 01-candidates.md has a dedicated 'Desired-Self / Identity-Projection Names' section with 7 entries (#155-161). expectations.text: Trend-Cycle Audit applied (will this name date?)
expectations.evidence: Transcript Step 02a section 6: 'Trend-Cycle Audit. "Will this name feel dated in 5 years?"' Includes detailed analysis across categories: one-syllable found words (TIMELESS), Japanese-inflected (DURABLE), 'The [Noun]' pattern (SATURATED - avoided), deliberately dark/confrontational (STILL STRONG), artisanal/craft-coded (NEUTRAL). Lists specific names that survive 2030. YAML lists 'trend_cycle_audit' in techniques_used.

expectations.text: Each riff labeled with which technique produced it
expectations.evidence: All 6 riff families in 01-candidates.md have a 'Technique' column in their tables. Every riff is individually labeled: e.g., Smelter = 'Metaphor shift: the tool that processes slag', Coke = 'Adjacent material: industrial fuel residue', Larva = 'Zoom in: moth's fabric-eating stage', Stray = 'Synonym explosion: feral = a stray'. The main generation candidates (#1-187) also have individual Technique column entries.

expectations.text: 10+ NEW UNIQUE riffs per direction (duplicates of existing candidates don't count)
expectations.evidence: Transcript Step 02c riff count verification table: Slag 13 new unique (0 duplicates), Moth 12 new unique (0 duplicates), Murk 11 new unique (0 duplicates), Feral 11 new unique (0 duplicates), Refuse 11 new unique (0 duplicates), Grime 11 new unique (1 duplicate). All 6 directions pass the >=10 threshold. 01-candidates.md confirms with numbered entries: Slag #188-200 (13), Moth #201-212 (12), Murk #213-223 (11), Feral #224-234 (11), Refuse #235-245 (11), Grime #246-256 (11). Total: 69 new unique riffs.

expectations.text: Consumer riffing checklist used (adjacent metaphor, sensory expansion, nature mining, French naturalized vocab)
expectations.evidence: Transcript Step 03a Phase 3 Exit Checkpoint: 'Consumer Riffing Checklist — ALL SATISFIED: [x] Adjacent metaphor — applied across all 6 riff families, [x] Sensory expansion — Five Senses Scan in 02a, [x] Nature mining — Territory 4 fully explored, [x] French naturalized vocabulary — Noir, Triage, Cache, Debris explored, [x] Shelf / Gift / Social-native tests — all applied during shortlisting.' 02-shortlist.md YAML confirms riffing_checklist with all 5 items true and status '5 of 5 core consumer techniques applied — SATISFIED'.

expectations.text: Hard gate checks displayed before generation menu
expectations.evidence: Transcript Step 02a shows 'Hard Gate Status (Post-Techniques)' with 4 checks all PASS: candidate count 187 (min 60), category coverage 11/11 (min 6), structural mix 7 types (min 3), technique coverage 25+ applied. Transcript Step 03a shows 'Phase 1: Entry Checkpoint — Hard Gate Checks — ALL PASS' with the same 4 checks at 256 candidates. These are displayed before the user decision menu.

expectations.text: Inline conflict flagging for known conflicts (warning emoji or text flag - substance over format)
expectations.evidence: No inline conflict flags appear anywhere in 01-candidates.md or 02-shortlist.md. Names like Fox, Crow, Moth, Forge, Stray, Rogue, Viper, Relic, Flux — many of which have known existing brands — are listed without any conflict notation. The only mention of conflict flagging is in the brief (Step 01a): 'Relax conflict flagging — fashion brands routinely share names across classes.' While relaxed flagging is appropriate for fashion, the assertion requires some flagging to exist, even if relaxed. No conflicts were flagged at all, not even for names with obvious existing brand uses.

expectations.text: Relaxed conflict-flagging per fashion guidance (a plumbing company conflict is irrelevant)
expectations.evidence: Transcript Step 01a explicitly states: 'Relax conflict flagging — fashion brands routinely share names across classes.' The brief (00-brief.md) does not list any conflict flags. The Pre-Mortem section addresses brand risk but specifically notes 'Diesel, Acne, Supreme survived controversy' — demonstrating awareness that fashion brands successfully coexist with same-named brands in other categories. The relaxed approach is correctly applied throughout.

expectations.text: At least 40 unique candidates generated
expectations.evidence: 01-candidates.md YAML: total_unique_candidates: 256. The file contains numbered entries from #1 to #256 (with a few noted duplicates like #95 Surplus and #119 Sumi). Even accounting for duplicates, the count vastly exceeds 40. The transcript confirms '187 unique candidates' from initial generation, growing to 256 after riffing.

Then 16 numbered techniques are listed. Transcript Step 02c 'Technique Toolkit (Updated)' shows applied/unapplied status: 6 consumer-specific techniques marked [x] with checkmarks and '(applied in 02a)' notes, plus '7 techniques remaining (optional at saturation depth)' listed by name. This demonstrates a toolkit with status tracking and user selection.

claims.evidence: 01-candidates.md contains numbered entries from #1 to #256. A few entries note duplicates (#95 Surplus = duplicate of #63, #119 Sumi = duplicate of #47, #185 Slab = duplicate of #112), so the actual unique count is approximately 253. The YAML frontmatter claims total_unique_candidates: 256. Close enough that the discrepancy is minor (3 noted duplicates out of 256).

The generation table in the transcript lists 11 categories: Found Word, Evocative, Sound-Symbolic/Coined, Arbitrary/Absurd, Sensory (Five Senses), Foreign-Root, Metaphorical, Verb-as-Brand, Invented/Coined, Lexical Blends, Contrarian/Cross-Domain. YAML confirms categories_not_attempted: [].

YAML techniques_used array contains 24 named techniques. The transcript claims 25+ with 7 remaining. Counting from the transcript's technique sections: Tree of Thought, Five Senses Scan, Prefix-Suffix Matrix, Morphological Analysis, Domain-First, Contrarian/Anti-Category, Desired-Self, Trend-Cycle Audit, Memetic/Viral, Cultural Tension, SCAMPER, Constraint Removal, Pre-Mortem, Inversion, Three-Team, Sensory Dissonance, Subculture Code-Switch, Adjacent-Category, Place/Environment Evocation, Ingredient/Process, Color-Name Mapping, Arbitrary/Absurd, Verb-as-Brand, Obscure Real Word Mining = 24. Slight shortfall from '25+' but close.

claims.claim: 69 new unique riffs generated across 6 directions
claims.evidence: Counting entries in 01-candidates.md: Slag (#188-200) = 13, Moth (#201-212) = 12, Murk (#213-223) = 11, Feral (#224-234) = 11, Refuse (#235-245) = 11, Grime (#246-256) = 11. Total = 69. One duplicate noted in Grime family, leaving 69 new unique riffs as claimed.

02-shortlist.md lists exactly 25 names in three tiers: Tier 1 (7 names: Slag, Dross, Moth, Feral, Murk, Raze, Grime), Tier 2 (11 names: Crow, Flux, Refuse, Cull, Soot, Forge, Vex, Stray, Fray, Loot, Char), Tier 3 (7 names: Kuro, Sumi, Surplus, Remnant, Offcut, Salvage, Smog). 7 + 11 + 7 = 25.

claims.claim: French naturalized vocabulary explored (Noir, Triage, Cache, Debris)
claims.evidence: 01-candidates.md: Noir (#35), Triage (#105), Cache (#108) appear as candidates. Debris (#243) appears in the Refuse riff family. All four are present in the outputs.

Adjacent metaphor: present in riff families (e.g., Vagrant for Feral, Gossamer for Moth). Sensory expansion: Five Senses Scan generated 12 candidates. Nature mining: Territory 4 has 15 animal/nature candidates. French naturalized: Noir, Triage, Cache, Debris. Shelf/Gift/Social tests: all documented in 02-shortlist.md with detailed results.
Searched all 256 candidates. No -works, -craft, -hub, -ify, -lab, -io compounds appear. Compounds present are fashion-appropriate: Slag Supply, Moth Archive, Dead Mall, Night Shift, etc.

suggestions.assertion: Inline conflict flagging for known conflicts
suggestions.reason: This assertion is in tension with the fashion-conflict-tolerance assertion. The skill guidance says to relax conflict flagging for fashion, but this assertion expects some flagging to exist. Consider clarifying: should relaxed flagging mean 'flag only same-category fashion conflicts' (e.g., Fox Racing for a Fox streetwear name) or 'flag nothing'? Currently a session could either pass both (flag lightly) or fail one (flag nothing vs flag everything). The intent may be better served by a single assertion: 'Conflict flagging is present but relaxed for cross-industry conflicts per fashion guidance.'

This threshold is trivially met (256 vs 40). The session generated 6.4x the minimum. Consider raising to at least 100 for a deep consumer session, or splitting into 'minimum 40 from initial generation' and 'minimum 80 total including riffs' to be more discriminating.

No assertion checks the quality or appropriateness of the final shortlist for the specific brief. A session could generate 256 generic names, shortlist 25 that don't match the streetwear/dark/rebellious brief, and still pass all assertions. Consider an assertion like 'Shortlisted names align with the brief's stated personality (rebellious, dark, urban)' or 'Shortlist contains no names that signal corporate/eco/pastoral energy.'

No assertion checks whether definitions/meanings are provided for shortlisted names. The shortlist includes words like Slag, Dross, Kuro, Sumi that many people wouldn't know. The 02-shortlist.md does include 'Why It's Here' rationale for each name, but this isn't tested.

eval_feedback.overall: Strong assertion set covering both workflow mechanics and consumer-specific techniques. The main gap is the conflict-flagging tension between two assertions that pull in opposite directions, and the absence of any quality-of-output assertion for brief alignment. The candidate count threshold is too low to be discriminating for a deep session.
